title: Iatrogenic Hypercobalaminemia
subtitle: When to Stop B12 Supplements — Pharmacokinetics, Washout Logic & the Endogenous Red Flag, Verified, With a Skepticism Section

items.title: Why is the decision to stop B12 supplements about physiology versus the laboratory printout?
items.content:
When managing iatrogenic hypercobalaminemia (elevated serum B12 from exogenous supplementation), the duration of cessation depends entirely on whether you are treating the patient's physiology or treating the laboratory printout.
From a first-principles pharmacokinetic perspective, there is no biological mandate to "wash out" excess B12 for safety reasons, as the molecule lacks a toxic ceiling. However, if the goal is to establish a true biochemical baseline, complete cessation for 4 to 8 weeks is the standard requirement.
keyPoints: No safety mandate to wash out excess B12 — no toxic ceiling in humans.
Stop 4–8 weeks only when you need a true biochemical baseline.
Clinical action should follow underlying cause, not reflexively chase a high lab flag.
items.reference: Core Principle: "there is no biological mandate to wash out excess B12 for safety reasons, as the molecule lacks a toxic ceiling... if the goal is to establish a true biochemical baseline, a complete cessation of 4 to 8 weeks is the standard requirement."

items.title: How does the liver act as a biological capacitor for vitamin B12?
items.content:
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is uniquely handled compared to other water-soluble vitamins because the liver acts as a massive biological capacitor.
A healthy liver stores between 2 to 5 mg of B12, while the body utilizes only about 1 to 3 µg per day — roughly 0.1% of total stores daily.
keyPoints: Hepatic stores: 2–5 mg in a healthy liver.
Daily utilization: only 1–3 µg/day (~0.1% of stores).
B12 behaves unlike typical water-soluble vitamins due to hepatic sequestration.
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "A healthy liver stores between 2 to 5 mg of B12... The body utilizes only about 1 to 3 µg per day (roughly 0.1% of total stores)."

items.title: Why does serum B12 normalize in weeks while hepatic stores remain saturated for years?
items.content:
The biological half-life of stored hepatic B12 is approximately 400 days.
When high-dose oral or intramuscular B12 is administered, transport proteins (Transcobalamin I, II, and III) become 100% saturated. Excess unbound B12 is rapidly filtered by the kidneys and excreted in urine. Circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks, but hepatic stores remain fully saturated for 1 to 3 years.
keyPoints: Hepatic t½ of stored B12: ~400 days.
Transcobalamins I, II, III saturate at high doses.
Serum excess clears in 4–6 weeks; hepatic saturation persists 1–3 years.
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "The biological half-life of stored B12 is approximately 400 days... The circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks, but the hepatic stores will remain fully saturated for 1 to 3 years."

items.title: How long must B12 be stopped to establish a true absorptive baseline?
items.content:
If a patient was empirically placed on high-dose B12 without a baseline lab and you now need an accurate assessment of endogenous absorptive capacity, stop all B12 intake for a minimum of 4 weeks, ideally 8 weeks.
Testing any sooner will only measure exogenous circulating excess, yielding a falsely reassuring result >1000 pg/mL.
keyPoints: Minimum washout: 4 weeks; ideal: 8 weeks.
Earlier testing measures supplement, not absorptive capacity.
Premature testing yields falsely reassuring levels >1000 pg/mL.
items.reference: Scenario A: "stop all B12 intake for a minimum of 4 weeks, ideally 8 weeks... Testing any sooner will only measure the exogenous circulating excess."

items.title: When can B12 supplementation be stopped entirely after correcting a reversible deficiency?
items.content:
For reversible causes — such as dietary veganism, corrected SIBO, or ceased prolonged PPI use — stop the supplement entirely once serum B12 reaches >800 pg/mL.
The liver has enough stores to last for over a year. Check serum B12 annually thereafter.
keyPoints: Reversible causes: vegan diet, corrected SIBO, stopped PPI.
Stop supplement when serum B12 >800 pg/mL.
Hepatic stores sustain physiology >1 year; recheck annually.
items.reference: Scenario B — Reversible: "Stop the supplement entirely once serum reaches >800 pg/mL. The liver has enough stores to last for over a year. Check serum B12 annually."

items.title: Why must B12 never be stopped in pernicious anemia or post-gastric bypass malabsorption?
items.content:
For irreversible causes — such as pernicious anemia, severe terminal ileitis, or gastric bypass — do not stop supplementation. A high serum B12 in these patients simply proves compliance.
If the level is >1500 pg/mL, do not mandate a washout. Instead, space out IM injections (e.g., from every 2 weeks to every 2–3 months) or reduce oral dose to 1000 µg daily maintenance.
keyPoints: Irreversible malabsorption: pernicious anemia, ileitis, gastric bypass.
High B12 = compliance marker, not toxicity.
If >1500 pg/mL: space IM injections or reduce to 1000 µg oral maintenance — do not wash out.
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "Do not stop. A high serum B12 in these patients simply proves compliance... simply space out the IM injections... or reduce the oral dose to a 1000 µg daily maintenance."

items.title: When should B12 be stopped for dermatologic side effects, and how long until resolution?
items.content:
While rare, excess circulating B12 can alter the skin microbiome — specifically promoting Cutibacterium acnes transcription — triggering inflammatory acne or rosacea-like eruptions.
If this occurs, stop supplementation immediately. The dermatological flare typically resolves within 2 to 4 weeks as renal clearance drops free circulating B12 levels.
keyPoints: Rare: B12 may promote C. acnes transcription → acne/rosacea-like eruption.
Stop supplement immediately if dermatologic flare occurs.
Resolution expected in 2–4 weeks as renal clearance lowers free B12.
items.reference: Scenario C: "excess circulating B12 can alter the skin microbiome (specifically promoting Cutibacterium acnes transcription)... stop the supplementation immediately... resolve within 2 to 4 weeks."

items.title: Why is reflexively stopping B12 for a high lab value often a clinical error?
items.content:
Academics and general practitioners often panic when B12 is flagged as "High" (e.g., >1500 pg/mL) and immediately halt therapy, warning of "toxicity."
There is zero high-quality evidence that exogenous B12 toxicity exists in humans — the body simply urinates out unbound excess. Treating the elevated lab value by initiating strict, prolonged washout in a patient with irreversible malabsorption only guarantees eventual return to megaloblastic anemia and peripheral neuropathy.
keyPoints: No high-quality evidence of exogenous B12 toxicity in humans.
Unbound excess is renally excreted.
Washout in irreversible malabsorption risks recurrent megaloblastic anemia and neuropathy.
items.reference: Academic Misconception: "There is zero high-quality evidence that exogenous B12 toxicity exists in humans... Treating the elevated lab value by initiating a strict, prolonged washout in a patient with an irreversible malabsorption syndrome only guarantees their eventual return to megaloblastic anemia."

items.title: When is a high B12 level dangerous because the patient is NOT taking supplements?
items.content:
The only time a high B12 level is dangerous is when the patient is NOT taking supplements.
If you stop the patient's B12 for 8 to 12 weeks and serum level remains paradoxically elevated (>1000 pg/mL), this is a major red flag for endogenous hypercobalaminemia — not iatrogenic excess.
keyPoints: Dangerous high B12 = endogenous, not supplement-related.
After 8–12 week washout, persistent level >1000 pg/mL is a red flag.
Do not dismiss as residual supplement effect without confirming supplement cessation.
items.reference: Skeptic's Trap: "The only time a high B12 level is dangerous is when the patient is NOT taking supplements... their serum level remains paradoxically elevated (>1000 pg/mL), this is a major red flag."

items.title: Why do myeloproliferative neoplasms cause endogenous hypercobalaminemia?
items.content:
Endogenous hypercobalaminemia is a marker of myeloproliferative neoplasms (e.g., CML, polycythemia vera) due to massive overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin) by granulocytes.
This raises total serum B12 independently of supplementation or hepatic release.
keyPoints: MPN (CML, PV) → granulocyte overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin).
Elevated haptocorrin-bound B12 raises total serum level.
Persists after supplement washout — warrants hematologic workup.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "Myeloproliferative Neoplasms: (e.g., CML, Polycythemia Vera), due to massive overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin) by granulocytes."

items.title: How does hepatic necrosis cause paradoxically elevated serum B12?
items.content:
In hepatic necrosis or severe liver disease, structural breakdown of the liver dumps its massive ~5 mg B12 reserve directly into the bloodstream, causing acute elevation of serum cobalamin unrelated to supplementation.
This must be distinguished from iatrogenic hypercobalaminemia after appropriate washout.
keyPoints: Hepatic necrosis releases stored hepatic B12 (~5 mg reserve) into circulation.
Causes acute serum elevation without ongoing supplementation.
Distinguish from supplement effect via history and liver evaluation.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "Hepatic Necrosis/Disease: The liver's structural breakdown dumps its massive 5 mg reserve directly into the bloodstream."

items.title: Which solid tumors are associated with endogenous hypercobalaminemia?
items.content:
Solid tumors — particularly hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) — can cause endogenous hypercobalaminemia.
Persistent elevation after confirmed supplement cessation should prompt evaluation for occult malignancy alongside MPN and liver disease.
keyPoints: HCC is a recognized cause of endogenous hypercobalaminemia.
Persistent high B12 after washout → evaluate MPN, liver disease, and malignancy.
Not all high B12 is benign supplement effect.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "Solid Tumors: Particularly Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC)."

items.title: How do Transcobalamin I, II, and III handle high-dose B12 administration?
items.content:
When high-dose oral or IM B12 is administered, transport proteins Transcobalamin I, II, and III become 100% saturated.
Only the fraction bound to Transcobalamin II is biologically active for cellular delivery; excess unbound and haptocorrin-bound B12 is rapidly cleared renally, explaining why serum can remain elevated while toxicity does not occur.
keyPoints: High-dose B12 saturates Transcobalamins I, II, and III.
TC II carries biologically active cobalamin to cells.
Unbound excess is rapidly filtered and excreted renally.
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "the transport proteins (Transcobalamin I, II, and III) become 100% saturated. Any excess unbound B12 is rapidly filtered by the kidneys and excreted in the urine."

items.title: Why is stopping B12 in documented severe deficiency a clinical error if the mechanism is uncorrected?
items.content:
If the patient had a documented, severe deficiency, stopping the supplement entirely is a clinical error when the underlying malabsorption mechanism has not been corrected.
The cause — not the lab value — dictates whether cessation, dose reduction, or continued lifelong replacement is appropriate.
keyPoints: Documented severe deficiency + uncorrected malabsorption → do not stop.
Underlying mechanism determines action, not serum level alone.
Dose spacing or reduction preferred over washout in irreversible disease.
items.reference: Scenario B: "If the patient had a documented, severe deficiency, stopping the supplement entirely is a clinical error if the underlying mechanism has not been corrected."

items.title: How should B12 >1500 pg/mL be managed in a compliant patient with pernicious anemia?
items.content:
In pernicious anemia or other irreversible malabsorption, B12 >1500 pg/mL does not require washout or cessation.
Adjust the regimen: space IM injections from every 2 weeks to every 2–3 months, or switch to 1000 µg oral daily maintenance if tolerated. The elevated level confirms adequate replacement, not toxicity.
keyPoints: >1500 pg/mL in PA: adjust dose, do not stop.
Space IM: q2 weeks → q2–3 months.
Alternative: 1000 µg oral daily maintenance.
High level = compliance/adequate replacement, not toxicity.
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "If the level is >1500 pg/mL, do not mandate a washout; simply space out the IM injections... or reduce the oral dose to a 1000 µg daily maintenance."

items.title: Why can a falsely reassuring B12 level persist for weeks after stopping supplements?
items.content:
Even after stopping exogenous B12, serum levels may remain >1000 pg/mL for 4 to 6 weeks due to saturated transcobalamin binding and gradual renal clearance of the circulating pool.
This is expected iatrogenic carryover — not evidence of endogenous production — unless elevation persists beyond 8 to 12 weeks of confirmed abstinence.
keyPoints: Serum B12 may stay >1000 pg/mL for 4–6 weeks after stopping supplements.
Expected due to saturated transport proteins and slow clearance.
Persistent elevation >8–12 weeks off supplements → investigate endogenous causes.
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math & Skeptic's Trap: "circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks... If you stop the patient's B12 for 8 to 12 weeks, and their serum level remains paradoxically elevated (>1000 pg/mL), this is a major red flag."

items.question: A 62-year-old woman on cyanocobalamin 1000 µg daily has serum B12 of 1850 pg/mL. She has no supplement-related symptoms. She had pernicious anemia diagnosed 3 years ago with prior macrocytic anemia and anti-intrinsic factor antibodies. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Stop all B12 immediately and recheck in 8 weeks to confirm toxicity resolution
Continue replacement but space injections or reduce to maintenance dose without washout
Switch to folic acid monotherapy until B12 normalizes
Obtain bone marrow biopsy to rule out MPN before any B12 adjustment
items.explanation: In irreversible malabsorption (pernicious anemia), high B12 confirms compliance, not toxicity. Adjust dose (space IM or reduce to 1000 µg maintenance) — do not wash out, which risks recurrent deficiency neuropathy.
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "Do not stop... If the level is >1500 pg/mL, do not mandate a washout; simply space out the IM injections."

items.question: A vegan patient was started empirically on oral B12 2000 µg daily without a baseline level. You need to assess true intrinsic absorptive capacity. How long should all B12 be withheld before testing?
options: 48 hours
1 week
Minimum 4 weeks, ideally 8 weeks
6 months to allow hepatic stores to deplete
items.explanation: Serum excess clears in 4–6 weeks; minimum 4 weeks (ideally 8 weeks) off all B12 is required before testing can reflect endogenous absorptive capacity rather than supplement carryover.
items.reference: Scenario A: "stop all B12 intake for a minimum of 4 weeks, ideally 8 weeks."

items.question: A patient denies any B12 supplements. After 10 weeks of confirmed abstinence, serum B12 remains 1420 pg/mL. Which workup is most appropriate?
options: Repeat level in 6 months; likely residual supplement effect
Increase oral B12 to confirm malabsorption
Evaluate for MPN, hepatic disease, and occult malignancy
Start folate supplementation to lower B12
items.explanation: Persistent elevation >1000 pg/mL after 8–12 weeks off supplements is endogenous hypercobalaminemia — evaluate MPN (haptocorrin overproduction), hepatic necrosis, and solid tumors such as HCC.
items.reference: Skeptic's Trap: "If you stop the patient's B12 for 8 to 12 weeks, and their serum level remains paradoxically elevated (>1000 pg/mL), this is a major red flag."

items.question: A patient developed B12 deficiency from prolonged omeprazole use. PPI was stopped 6 months ago, diet improved, and serum B12 is now 920 pg/mL on 1000 µg daily cyanocobalamin. Next step?
options: Stop supplement; recheck B12 annually
Continue lifelong high-dose B12 regardless of level
Obtain anti-intrinsic factor antibodies before any change
Switch to IM B12 monthly indefinitely
items.explanation: Reversible cause (ceased PPI) with B12 >800 pg/mL: stop supplement. Hepatic stores last >1 year; annual monitoring suffices.
items.reference: Scenario B — Reversible: "Stop the supplement entirely once serum reaches >800 pg/mL... Check serum B12 annually."

items.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between serum B12 clearance and hepatic B12 stores after high-dose supplementation?
options: Serum and hepatic stores clear at the same rate (~400-day half-life)
Serum excess clears in 4–6 weeks; hepatic stores remain saturated 1–3 years
Hepatic stores clear within days; serum remains elevated for years
Both clear completely within 2 weeks of stopping supplements
items.explanation: Circulating excess washes out in 4–6 weeks via renal clearance of saturated transcobalamins, but hepatic capacitor stores remain fully saturated for 1–3 years (hepatic t½ ~400 days).
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks, but the hepatic stores will remain fully saturated for 1 to 3 years."

items.question: A patient on high-dose oral B12 develops new inflammatory acne 3 weeks after starting supplementation. B12 level is 1680 pg/mL. Best management?
options: Continue B12; acne is unrelated to supplementation
Stop B12; expect dermatologic improvement in 2–4 weeks
Add isotretinoin and continue B12 for neuropathy prevention
Switch to folic acid 5 mg daily as B12 alternative
items.explanation: Rare B12-associated acne/rosacea from altered skin microbiome (C. acnes). Stop supplement immediately; flare typically resolves in 2–4 weeks as free circulating B12 clears.
items.reference: Scenario C: "stop the supplementation immediately. The dermatological flare will typically resolve within 2 to 4 weeks."

items.question: Which statement about exogenous B12 "toxicity" is most accurate?
options: Levels >1500 pg/mL require immediate cessation in all patients
High-dose B12 causes irreversible neurologic toxicity in most patients
No high-quality evidence of exogenous B12 toxicity exists; excess is renally excreted
Hepatic failure is common with chronic oral B12 >1000 µg daily
items.explanation: There is zero high-quality evidence of exogenous B12 toxicity in humans. Unbound excess is urinated out. Reflex washout in malabsorption syndromes risks recurrent deficiency complications.
items.reference: Academic Misconception: "There is zero high-quality evidence that exogenous B12 toxicity exists in humans. The body simply urinates out the unbound excess."

items.question: Polycythemia vera causes elevated serum B12 primarily through:
options: Increased dietary B12 absorption in the gut
Massive hepatic B12 release from cirrhosis
Overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin) by granulocytes
Direct cobalamin synthesis by malignant erythroid precursors
items.explanation: MPNs such as PV cause endogenous hypercobalaminemia via granulocyte overproduction of haptocorrin (Transcobalamin I), raising total serum B12 independently of supplementation.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "massive overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin) by granulocytes."

items.question: A gastric bypass patient on IM cyanocobalamin q2 weeks has B12 of 2100 pg/mL, no symptoms, and documented prior deficiency. Most appropriate adjustment?
options: Stop all B12 for 8 weeks to establish baseline
Space IM injections to every 2–3 months or use 1000 µg oral daily
Reduce to 100 µg oral daily and recheck in 1 week
Add folic acid to accelerate B12 clearance
items.explanation: Irreversible malabsorption post-bypass: high B12 proves compliance. Do not wash out — space IM to q2–3 months or use 1000 µg oral maintenance.
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "simply space out the IM injections (e.g., from every 2 weeks to every 2–3 months) or reduce the oral dose to a 1000 µg daily maintenance."

items.question: Daily body utilization of stored vitamin B12 is approximately:
options: 1–3 µg/day (~0.1% of hepatic stores)
50–100 µg/day (~5% of hepatic stores)
500 µg/day (~25% of hepatic stores)
2–5 mg/day (matching total hepatic storage)
items.explanation: The body uses only 1–3 µg/day against hepatic stores of 2–5 mg — roughly 0.1% of total stores daily — explaining why hepatic reserves last over a year after repletion.
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "The body utilizes only about 1 to 3 µg per day (roughly 0.1% of total stores)."

items.statement: After high-dose B12 supplementation, circulating serum excess typically clears within 4 to 6 weeks while hepatic stores may remain saturated for 1 to 3 years.
items.explanation: Renal clearance of saturated transcobalamins removes serum excess in weeks; hepatic capacitor stores deplete much more slowly (t½ ~400 days).
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks, but the hepatic stores will remain fully saturated for 1 to 3 years."

items.statement: Exogenous vitamin B12 has a well-documented toxic ceiling requiring routine washout in all patients with levels >1500 pg/mL.
items.explanation: No high-quality evidence of exogenous B12 toxicity exists. Excess unbound B12 is renally excreted. Washout is for baseline testing or side effects — not universal toxicity prevention.
items.reference: Academic Misconception: "There is zero high-quality evidence that exogenous B12 toxicity exists in humans."

items.statement: In pernicious anemia, a serum B12 >1500 pg/mL on replacement therapy should prompt immediate cessation of all B12.
items.explanation: High B12 in PA proves compliance. Adjust dose (space IM or reduce to maintenance) — do not stop, which risks recurrent megaloblastic anemia and neuropathy.
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "Do not stop... do not mandate a washout."

items.statement: Persistent serum B12 >1000 pg/mL after 8–12 weeks off all supplements suggests endogenous hypercobalaminemia.
items.explanation: After adequate washout, persistent elevation is a red flag for MPN, hepatic necrosis, or malignancy — not residual supplement effect.
items.reference: Skeptic's Trap: "their serum level remains paradoxically elevated (>1000 pg/mL), this is a major red flag."

items.statement: After correcting a reversible B12 deficiency (e.g., stopped PPI), supplementation can be stopped once serum B12 exceeds 800 pg/mL.
items.explanation: Hepatic stores sustain physiology >1 year after repletion. Annual monitoring is sufficient for reversible causes once adequately replete.
items.reference: Scenario B — Reversible: "Stop the supplement entirely once serum reaches >800 pg/mL."

items.statement: B12-associated acne or rosacea-like eruptions typically resolve within 2–4 weeks after stopping supplementation.
items.explanation: Stopping B12 allows renal clearance of free circulating cobalamin; dermatologic flare from altered C. acnes microbiome typically resolves in 2–4 weeks.
items.reference: Scenario C: "The dermatological flare will typically resolve within 2 to 4 weeks."

items.statement: Hepatic necrosis can cause elevated serum B12 by releasing the liver's stored cobalamin reserve into the bloodstream.
items.explanation: Structural liver breakdown dumps the ~5 mg hepatic B12 reserve into circulation, causing acute elevation unrelated to supplementation.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "The liver's structural breakdown dumps its massive 5 mg reserve directly into the bloodstream."

items.statement: Testing serum B12 within 2 weeks of stopping high-dose supplements reliably reflects endogenous absorptive capacity.
items.explanation: Testing before 4 weeks (ideally 8) measures exogenous circulating excess, yielding falsely reassuring levels >1000 pg/mL.
items.reference: Scenario A: "Testing any sooner will only measure the exogenous circulating excess."

items.assertion: Patients with iatrogenic hypercobalaminemia do not require B12 washout for safety reasons alone.
items.reason: Vitamin B12 lacks a documented toxic ceiling in humans and excess unbound cobalamin is renally excreted.
items.explanation: Both true; washout is indicated for baseline testing or side effects, not reflexive toxicity concern.
items.reference: Core Principle: "no biological mandate to wash out excess B12 for safety reasons, as the molecule lacks a toxic ceiling."

items.assertion: A high serum B12 in a patient with pernicious anemia on replacement therapy indicates adequate compliance.
items.reason: Exogenous B12 toxicity causes progressive peripheral neuropathy requiring immediate cessation.
items.explanation: A is true (high B12 = compliance in PA); R is false (no evidence of exogenous B12 toxicity causing neuropathy — stopping causes deficiency neuropathy).
items.reference: Scenario B — Irreversible: "A high serum B12 in these patients simply proves compliance"; Academic Misconception: "zero high-quality evidence that exogenous B12 toxicity exists."

items.assertion: Myeloproliferative neoplasms can cause elevated serum B12 without supplementation.
items.reason: Granulocytes overproduce Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin), raising total serum cobalamin.
items.explanation: Both true; MPN-associated haptocorrin overproduction is a classic cause of endogenous hypercobalaminemia.
items.reference: Endogenous Hypercobalaminemia: "massive overproduction of Transcobalamin I (haptocorrin) by granulocytes."

items.assertion: Serum B12 may remain elevated for weeks after stopping oral supplementation.
items.reason: Hepatic B12 stores are depleted within days of stopping supplements.
items.explanation: A is true (serum clears in 4–6 weeks); R is false (hepatic stores remain saturated 1–3 years; t½ ~400 days).
items.reference: Pharmacokinetic Math: "circulating serum excess washes out in roughly 4 to 6 weeks, but the hepatic stores will remain fully saturated for 1 to 3 years."

items.assertion: B12 supplementation can be discontinued in a vegan who corrected dietary deficiency once serum B12 exceeds 800 pg/mL.
items.reason: Hepatic stores of 2–5 mg can sustain daily needs of 1–3 µg for over a year.
items.explanation: Both true; reversible deficiency with adequate repletion allows cessation with annual monitoring.
items.reference: Scenario B — Reversible: "Stop the supplement entirely once serum reaches >800 pg/mL. The liver has enough stores to last for over a year."

items.assertion: A high B12 level is most concerning when the patient denies taking any supplements.
items.reason: Iatrogenic hypercobalaminemia always indicates occult malignancy.
items.explanation: A is true (endogenous elevation is the dangerous scenario); R is false (iatrogenic high B12 is common and benign; endogenous causes include MPN, liver disease, and HCC — not always malignancy, and iatrogenic is not malignant).
items.reference: Skeptic's Trap: "The only time a high B12 level is dangerous is when the patient is NOT taking supplements."

items.assertion: An 8-week B12 washout is preferred before assessing intrinsic absorptive capacity.
items.reason: Transcobalamin transport proteins remain saturated for weeks after high-dose supplementation.
items.explanation: Both true; saturated transcobalamins explain why shorter washouts yield falsely elevated serum levels.
items.reference: Scenario A: "ideally 8 weeks"; Pharmacokinetic Math: "transport proteins... become 100% saturated."

items.assertion: Inflammatory acne developing after starting high-dose B12 warrants stopping supplementation.
items.reason: Excess B12 permanently alters skin microbiome requiring lifelong dermatologic therapy.
items.explanation: A is true (stop supplement for dermatologic flare); R is false (flare typically resolves in 2–4 weeks after stopping — not permanent).
items.reference: Scenario C: "stop the supplementation immediately... resolve within 2 to 4 weeks."
